scope: Paediatrics stragglers L1+L2 -> L3 or discard (10 questions)

verdicts.new_stem: A 16-year-old boy is seen in general practice with a 6-month history of being unable to fall asleep until 0200 to 0300 despite being in bed from 2200, severely impaired wake on school mornings at 0630, and missing first period most days. On weekends and during recent school holidays he sleeps from approximately 0300 to 1200 and wakes refreshed and functioning well. He uses his phone in bed, denies caffeine after midday, denies illicit substance use, denies snoring or witnessed apnoeas, and a HEEADSSS screen is otherwise reassuring with no self-harm or pervasive low mood. His mother brought him today after two unsuccessful attempts at simply enforcing an earlier bedtime over the past month. He is otherwise well, on no medications, and his examination is unremarkable. A sleep diary over the past 14 days confirms a stable late sleep onset with normal total sleep duration when free running. You diagnose delayed sleep-wake phase disorder and discuss management with him and his mother.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which initial management plan is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Sleep hygiene counselling alone, with planned review in 6 weeks before considering any pharmacological or light-based measure
new_options.rationale: Sleep hygiene alone is insufficient once delayed sleep-wake phase disorder is established with impaired daytime function; behavioural plus chronotherapeutic measures are required from the outset, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia and RCH CPG: Sleep problems.

new_options.text: Low-dose melatonin 0.5 mg taken 5 to 7 hours before current sleep onset, with morning bright light and gradual schedule advance
new_options.rationale: Phase-advancing low-dose melatonin (0.3 to 0.5 mg) taken several hours before current sleep onset, combined with morning bright light and gradual schedule advance, is the evidence-based first-line regimen in Australian adolescent practice. The timing (well before bedtime, not at bedtime) and low dose are the levers that distinguish this from sleep-onset insomnia treatment, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia and RCH CPG: Sleep problems.

new_options.text: Melatonin 3 mg at the desired bedtime of 2200 with screen reduction advice and review in 4 weeks
new_options.rationale: Higher-dose melatonin at desired bedtime acts as a hypnotic rather than a phase-shifter; the chronobiotic effect requires a low dose taken hours before current sleep onset, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia.

new_options.text: Trial of temazepam at 2200 for 2 weeks to entrain an earlier sleep onset, then taper while consolidating sleep hygiene
new_options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not indicated for circadian phase disorders in adolescents and risk dependence and rebound; they do not shift the circadian phase, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia.

new_options.text: Referral for cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as the sole initial intervention before any light or melatonin trial
new_options.rationale: CBT-I targets sleep-onset and maintenance insomnia, not a phase-delayed circadian rhythm; the primary lever is phase shifting (bright light AM, low-dose melatonin PM) with behavioural support, per Sleep Health Foundation Australia.

verdicts.new_stem: A 13-year-old boy is seen at a community child and adolescent mental health service after 18 months of escalating behaviour: repeated school fights, theft from a local shop, deliberate fire-setting in a school rubbish bin, cruelty to the family cat on multiple occasions, breaking into a neighbour's shed and damaging tools, lying, breaking curfew, and overnight absconding. He shows little remorse. He has had episodes of defiance and rule-refusal since age 6, but the present behaviours represent a clear escalation. There is no documented substance use disorder, no psychotic features, no manic features, and no significant trauma history disclosed. He has no intellectual disability and attends mainstream Year 8 inconsistently. He is up to date with immunisations and his physical examination is normal. He meets DSM-5 criteria for adolescent-onset conduct disorder. His parents are exhausted, his school principal has indicated that one more incident will result in expulsion, and he and his parents have agreed to engage with treatment.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which first-line evidence-based intervention is most appropriate to recommend?

new_options.text: Commence risperidone 0.5 mg twice daily to target aggression and impulsivity, with monitoring of weight and metabolic parameters
new_options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line for conduct disorder and are reserved for severe persistent aggression unresponsive to psychosocial intervention, given significant metabolic and movement adverse effects, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines and RCH CPG: Behaviour.

new_options.text: Refer for structured family-based behavioural intervention (parent management training or multisystemic therapy); screen and treat comorbid ADHD, mood, substance use
new_options.rationale: Australian (RANZCP) and international evidence support structured family- and parent-based behavioural interventions (parent management training, multisystemic therapy, functional family therapy) as first-line for conduct disorder, alongside active screening and treatment of comorbid ADHD, mood, substance use and trauma, which co-occur in the majority of cases and worsen prognosis if missed, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines and headspace Clinical Toolkit.

new_options.text: Recommend a boot-camp style residential discipline programme to interrupt the current escalating behavioural pattern in a structured environment
new_options.rationale: Boot-camp and Scared Straight programmes have shown null or harmful effects on conduct disorder outcomes in randomised studies and are explicitly not recommended, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines. This is the anti-pattern tested here.

new_options.text: Individual psychodynamic psychotherapy weekly for 12 months as the sole intervention without parent or systemic involvement
new_options.rationale: Individual psychotherapy without family and systemic components is less effective than parent- and family-based interventions for conduct disorder in adolescents, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines.

new_options.text: Mandatory notification to police and child protection only, deferring any mental-health intervention until the legal pathway concludes fully
new_options.rationale: Engagement with justice and protective services where indicated does not replace evidence-based mental health treatment; delaying intervention worsens prognosis and is not consistent with stepped-care pathways, per RANZCP Practice Guidelines.

verdicts.new_stem: A 14-month-old fully immunised boy is reviewed in suburban general practice after three days of high fever to 39.7 deg C that resolved overnight. He is now afebrile and playful with a blanching, maculopapular, trunk-predominant rash that appeared this morning, sparing face, palms and soles. He has had no cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, oral lesions, or diarrhoea. There is no lymphadenopathy and no meningism, capillary refill is under two seconds, and mucous membranes are moist. He attends family daycare, where a 4-year-old sibling of another child has confirmed measles diagnosed yesterday by the public health unit and other children with similar fever-then-rash presentations have been seen at this practice in the past fortnight. The MMR vaccine is due at 18 months and has not yet been given. There is no household member who is immunocompromised or pregnant. The mother asks about diagnosis, time off daycare, and whether her son needs anything more.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of diagnosis and exclusion advice is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Roseola infantum; he may return to family daycare once afebrile and well, with no formal childcare exclusion period required under Australian guidance
new_options.rationale: Fever-then-rash in a well 6 to 24-month-old (trunk-predominant blanching rash after defervescence) is classical roseola (HHV-6). Under NHMRC Staying Healthy and RCH guidance, roseola does not require formal childcare exclusion; exclusion ends once the child is afebrile and well, per NHMRC Staying Healthy and RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

new_options.text: Measles given the daycare contact; exclude from family daycare for 4 days from rash onset and notify the public health unit
new_options.rationale: Measles requires fever PLUS cough, coryza or conjunctivitis with a cephalocaudal rash, none of which are present here; the well, post-defervescence trunk-predominant rash is roseola. Notification is required for measles but is not appropriate here, per NCIRS: Measles fact sheet and RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

new_options.text: Rubella; exclude from family daycare for 7 days from rash onset and arrange confirmatory serology before any return
new_options.rationale: Rubella shows tender suboccipital and post-auricular lymphadenopathy with a face-first cephalocaudal rash, not the trunk-predominant post-defervescence pattern seen here, per RCH CPG: Rubella.

new_options.text: Scarlet fever; commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg twice daily for 10 days and exclude from daycare for 24 hours after starting antibiotics
new_options.rationale: Scarlet fever has a sandpaper-textured rash with strawberry tongue, exudative pharyngitis and circumoral pallor; none are present and antibiotics are not indicated, per eTG: Group A streptococcal infection.

new_options.text: Roseola infantum; commence oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg four times daily for 5 days and exclude from daycare for the duration of treatment
new_options.rationale: Roseola is self-limiting; antivirals are not indicated in immunocompetent infants, and there is no daycare-exclusion requirement beyond being afebrile and well, per RCH CPG: Viral exanthems and NHMRC Staying Healthy.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-year-old unimmunised boy is brought to a regional Victorian emergency department with four days of fever to 39.8 deg C, barking moist cough, profuse coryza, and red watery photophobic eyes. He returned 10 days ago from a 3-week family trip to a rural province in Vietnam. He has no skin rash yet. On examination he is miserable with bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, clear nasal discharge, and several 1 to 2 mm bluish-white papules on an erythematous base on the buccal mucosa opposite the second molars. Temperature 39.6 deg C, HR 138, RR 32, SpO2 97 percent room air. He has no rash, no lymphadenopathy, chest is clear, and he is haemodynamically stable. He is currently in an open paediatric ED bay alongside other waiting children, including a 6-month-old infant and a pregnant accompanying parent. You strongly suspect measles based on Koplik spots and the three Cs.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which next step is most appropriate before laboratory confirmation?

new_options.text: Move him to a negative-pressure single room with airborne and contact precautions, and phone the public health unit to begin notification now
new_options.rationale: Measles is a notifiable disease in all Australian jurisdictions and clinical suspicion (especially with Koplik spots and an epidemiological link) triggers immediate isolation in a negative-pressure room with airborne and contact precautions and same-day phone notification to the public health unit BEFORE laboratory confirmation, to enable rapid contact tracing and PEP within the windows (MMR within 72 h, NHIG within 7 days), per CDNA: Measles SoNG and NCIRS: Measles fact sheet.

new_options.text: Send measles IgM and PCR (throat swab and urine) and await laboratory confirmation before notifying public health or isolating
new_options.rationale: Waiting for laboratory confirmation before isolation and notification breaches Australian public health practice and delays contact tracing and post-exposure prophylaxis for susceptible contacts (the infant and pregnant parent in the bay), per CDNA: Measles SoNG.

new_options.text: Place him in a standard single room with droplet precautions only and await senior review before any notification action
new_options.rationale: Measles is airborne; droplet precautions alone are inadequate and a negative-pressure room with N95 respirator entry is required, per CDNA: Measles SoNG.

new_options.text: Administer stat MMR vaccine and oral vitamin A 200 000 IU now, then discharge with isolation advice and review tomorrow
new_options.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in active suspected measles infection; vitamin A is recommended in confirmed cases of severe measles but does not replace isolation, notification, and inpatient assessment, and discharge before notification is unsafe, per WHO Measles guidelines and CDNA: Measles SoNG.

new_options.text: Commence empirical IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and admit for full sepsis workup including lumbar puncture before any isolation measures
new_options.rationale: The clinical picture is classical measles, not bacterial sepsis or meningitis; antibiotics and LP are not indicated and isolation plus notification take priority, per RCH CPG: Febrile child and CDNA: Measles SoNG.

verdicts.new_stem: A 7-year-old previously well Aboriginal girl from a remote community in the Northern Territory is reviewed during a paediatric outreach clinic in her community with a 2-day history of fever, sore throat and a rash that began today on the neck and chest before spreading to the trunk. She has no cough, coryza, or conjunctivitis. Temperature 38.6 deg C, HR 110, RR 22, SpO2 99 percent room air, capillary refill under two seconds. On examination she has a fine, blanching, erythematous papular eruption with a sandpaper texture accentuated in the antecubital and inguinal flexures, prominent fungiform papillae against a coated tongue, exudative tonsillitis, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and circumoral pallor. She has no known drug allergies, is fully immunised, has no history of acute rheumatic fever, and the family confirms she will be staying in community for at least the next two weeks. Throat swab for group A streptococcus has been collected. There is no current penicillin shortage in the community pharmacy.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which first-line antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg (up to 500 mg) twice daily for 10 days, with community follow-up at completion
new_options.rationale: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 10 days is first-line in non-high-ARF-risk Australian settings, but in high-ARF-risk settings (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children in remote/regional areas, Maori and Pacific children) the preferred regimen is a single dose of IM benzathine benzylpenicillin to ensure compliance and prevent acute rheumatic fever, per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline and eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis.

new_options.text: Single IM benzathine benzylpenicillin (900 mg if 20 kg or more, 450 mg if under 20 kg) administered now
new_options.rationale: In an Aboriginal child from a high-ARF-risk remote setting with clinical scarlet fever (group A streptococcal pharyngitis with toxin-mediated exanthem), a single dose of IM benzathine benzylpenicillin is the recommended Australian regimen to guarantee adherence and reduce ARF and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis risk, per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline and eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis.

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg once daily for 10 days as a more palatable equivalent to first-line oral penicillin
new_options.rationale: Amoxicillin is acceptable where penicillin V is unpalatable, but in high-ARF-risk settings an IM regimen is preferred to remove adherence as a failure mode, per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

new_options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg (up to 1 g) twice daily for 10 days as initial therapy
new_options.rationale: Cefalexin is broader-spectrum than necessary and reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy; it is not first-line in either standard or high-ARF-risk Australian settings, per eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis.

new_options.text: Symptomatic care with paracetamol and reassurance, with antibiotics only if the throat swab returns positive in 48 hours
new_options.rationale: Clinical scarlet fever with high ARF risk warrants immediate empirical antibiotics; awaiting culture results delays prevention of ARF and is not consistent with Australian guidance, per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

verdicts.new_stem: A 22-month-old fully immunised girl is reviewed in general practice with three days of low-grade fever to 38.4 deg C, drooling, and reduced oral intake. Her mother noticed today that she has spots on her hands, feet, and inside her mouth. On examination she is alert and well-perfused. Temperature 37.6 deg C, HR 116, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent room air. There are shallow ulcers on the tongue, buccal mucosa, and posterior pharynx, and several small grey-roofed oval vesicles on an erythematous base on the palms, soles, and dorsa of the hands and feet. There is no lip clustering, no gingival inflammation, and no lymphadenopathy. The nappy area shows a few similar vesicles. Mucous membranes are slightly dry but she has just taken 50 mL of cold water in clinic. There is a known outbreak of vesicular illness at her childcare centre that includes one recent admission to the local children's hospital. You diagnose hand-foot-and-mouth disease. Her mother asks what to do about childcare attendance and what warning signs would warrant urgent return.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of childcare exclusion advice and red-flag escalation criteria is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Exclude from childcare until all blisters have dried; return urgently for vomiting, ataxia, myoclonus, or reduced wet nappies
new_options.rationale: Australian guidance (NHMRC Staying Healthy, RCH) recommends childcare exclusion until all blisters have dried (not just until afebrile). Red flags for severe enterovirus disease (especially EV71) include neurological signs (ataxia, myoclonus, irritability), persistent high fever beyond 3 days, persistent vomiting, and inability to maintain hydration, all of which warrant urgent reassessment, per NHMRC Staying Healthy and RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

new_options.text: Exclude from childcare only until afebrile for 24 hours, then return regardless of any remaining skin lesions
new_options.rationale: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease requires exclusion until blisters have dried under NHMRC Staying Healthy, not merely until afebrile; the lesions remain shedding virus, per NHMRC Staying Healthy.

new_options.text: No childcare exclusion required as HFMD is self-limiting; return whenever the child is willing and able
new_options.rationale: Australian public health guidance does specify formal exclusion until blisters dry for hand-foot-and-mouth disease; no-exclusion advice is incorrect, per NHMRC Staying Healthy.

new_options.text: Commence oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg four times daily for 5 days and exclude from childcare for the duration of treatment
new_options.rationale: Aciclovir has no role in enteroviral HFMD; the diagnosis is not herpes simplex and antivirals are not indicated, per eTG: Herpes simplex and RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

new_options.text: Admit now for IV fluids and serial neurological observations, given the childcare outbreak link to EV71
new_options.rationale: She is clinically well, alert, perfused, and tolerating oral fluids in clinic; routine admission is not required and supportive outpatient care with explicit red-flag advice is appropriate, per RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old early childhood teacher at 18 weeks of her second pregnancy attends her GP after multiple confirmed cases of erythema infectiosum (slapped cheek) in the kindergarten class she teaches over the past fortnight. She had close prolonged indoor contact with two affected children one week ago, before they were excluded with their characteristic facial erythema and lacy peripheral rash. She is asymptomatic with no rash, arthralgia or fever. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated and her 4-year-old is in her class. Her 18-week morphology scan last week showed structurally normal anatomy with appropriate growth and amniotic fluid volume, and her first-trimester combined screen was low risk. She has no documented past clinical history of slapped cheek and no parvovirus B19 serology on file. Vital signs are normal, fundal height is consistent with dates, and her physical examination is unremarkable. She specifically asks whether she needs immediate fetal medicine referral or whether further blood tests come first.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which next investigation or referral is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Maternal parvovirus B19 IgG and IgM serology now, with further action determined by the IgG and IgM pattern
new_options.rationale: Australian (RANZCOG) and KEMH guidance: serology first is the AU-recommended pathway. IgG positive / IgM negative confirms pre-existing immunity (around 50 percent of women) and no further action is needed. IgG and IgM both negative means susceptible: repeat serology in 4 weeks. IgM positive (or IgG seroconversion) is confirmed recent infection and triggers MFM referral for serial fetal ultrasound and middle cerebral artery (MCA) Doppler velocimetry for at least 8 to 12 weeks, per RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy and KEMH: Parvovirus B19.

new_options.text: Immediate referral to maternal fetal medicine for weekly MCA Doppler velocimetry from now for 12 weeks regardless of serology
new_options.rationale: MCA Doppler surveillance is the correct AFTER-confirmation step but is unnecessary if she is already immune (around 50 percent prevalence) or if serology shows no infection; routine empirical Doppler before serology overcommits resources and is not the AU-recommended pathway, per RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy.

new_options.text: Urgent amniocentesis with parvovirus B19 PCR on amniotic fluid now to confirm or exclude vertical fetal transmission
new_options.rationale: Amniocentesis is reserved for confirmed maternal infection with evidence of fetal compromise; it is invasive and inappropriate as a first-line investigation in an asymptomatic exposed contact, per RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy.

new_options.text: Intramuscular normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) administered within 7 days of exposure as parvovirus post-exposure prophylaxis
new_options.rationale: NHIG is not indicated for parvovirus B19 exposure in pregnancy; there is no proven prophylactic benefit and current Australian guidance recommends serology and surveillance instead, per Australian Immunisation Handbook.

new_options.text: Reassure and discharge as parvovirus B19 has no documented effect on a pregnancy past 12 weeks gestation
new_options.rationale: Maternal parvovirus B19 infection can cause fetal anaemia and hydrops fetalis with the highest risk in the second trimester (around 9 to 20 weeks); discharge without serology at 18 weeks is unsafe, per RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy.

verdicts.new_stem: A 9-year-old boy on maintenance chemotherapy for B-cell ALL (14 months into therapy) is brought to a metropolitan paediatric ED 2 hours after a single recorded oral temperature of 38.4 deg C at home. He has had mild coryza but no localising features, no rigors, no rash, and no diarrhoea. He took oral paracetamol 1 hour ago. He has a tunnelled central venous access device with no overlying erythema or tenderness, and no recent admissions. He is fully immunised for age and has no known drug allergies. On arrival his temperature is 37.9 deg C, HR 116, RR 22, SpO2 98 percent room air, BP 104/64, capillary refill 2 seconds, and he looks well and is interacting normally. An FBC taken 3 days ago at clinic showed Hb 102 g/L, WCC 1.2, neutrophils 0.3 x10^9/L, platelets 138, and a repeat is in progress now. He meets the RCH definition of febrile neutropenia (single temperature 38.0 deg C or higher with ANC under 0.5). Peripheral and central line cultures are being collected.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which empirical antibiotic regimen and timing target is most appropriate?

new_options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam within 60 minutes of arrival, adding vancomycin only if shock, suspected line infection, severe mucositis, or known resistant colonisation
new_options.rationale: Australian paediatric oncology guidance (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia, eviQ) recommends empirical anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam monotherapy (piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime) within 60 minutes of arrival for low-risk well-appearing febrile neutropenia. Empirical vancomycin is reserved for shock, suspected central-line infection, severe mucositis or skin/soft tissue infection, or known resistant Gram-positive colonisation, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and eviQ Paediatric Fever and neutropenia.

new_options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily empirically within 60 minutes of arrival, with no anti-pseudomonal cover added
new_options.rationale: Ceftriaxone has inadequate anti-pseudomonal activity for empirical paediatric febrile neutropenia; an anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) is required, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

new_options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus empirical IV vancomycin and IV gentamicin within 60 minutes given the indwelling central line in situ
new_options.rationale: Empirical vancomycin and dual Gram-negative cover are not routine; the line is uninflamed and the patient is haemodynamically stable. Routine triple cover increases adverse effects without outcome benefit, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and eviQ.

new_options.text: Withhold antibiotics pending the repeat FBC to confirm ongoing neutropenia and pending the blood culture preliminary results
new_options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics within 60 minutes are mandatory; waiting for repeat FBC or culture results delays treatment of a potentially life-threatening sepsis, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

new_options.text: Discharge home on oral amoxicillin-clavulanate plus ciprofloxacin with outpatient FBC review tomorrow, given he is well and afebrile
new_options.rationale: Oral outpatient regimens for low-risk febrile neutropenia are an option only after a period of inpatient observation, stratification, and confirmed culture-negative trajectory in selected protocols; discharge from the ED on oral antibiotics at first contact is unsafe, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

verdicts.new_stem: A male infant born at 39+0 weeks by elective caesarean section without labour is admitted to the special care nursery at 1 hour of life for ongoing respiratory distress. Birthweight 3520 g, APGARs 8 and 9. He developed tachypnoea, mild grunting and intercostal recession from 15 minutes of age. He is now 4 hours old on nasal high-flow at 6 L/min with FiO2 0.30, maintaining SpO2 96 percent preductal and 96 percent postductal. Mother had a negative group B Strep screen, no fever, no prolonged rupture of membranes, no diabetes and no anaesthetic complications; no meconium-stained liquor. T 36.9 deg C, HR 152, RR 78, BP 62/40 with capillary refill 2 seconds and warm peripheries. Chest is clear with no asymmetry. Chest radiograph at 30 minutes showed hyperinflation, prominent perihilar streaking, fluid in the right horizontal fissure, and no air bronchograms or focal consolidation. He has not yet fed. Blood gas: pH 7.32, pCO2 48 mmHg, BE -2. Sepsis bloods are pending. You are confident this is transient tachypnoea of the newborn (TTN).
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which next management step is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Continue nasal high-flow titrated to SpO2 91-95 percent, nil by mouth on IV maintenance, reassess at 72 hours
new_options.rationale: TTN is managed supportively with non-invasive respiratory support (nasal high-flow or CPAP) titrated to keep SpO2 91 to 95 percent, gut rest until work of breathing settles, and IV maintenance fluids while waiting for fetal lung fluid to clear (usually within 24 to 72 hours). Persisting tachypnoea beyond 72 hours mandates reconsideration of the diagnosis (sepsis, cardiac, surfactant deficiency), per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress and Safer Care Victoria neonatal eHandbook.

new_options.text: Administer endotracheal surfactant via the INSURE technique now, given the persisting FiO2 requirement at 4 hours of postnatal life
new_options.rationale: Surfactant therapy is for respiratory distress syndrome (preterm, ground-glass with air bronchograms, surfactant deficiency); the CXR pattern and term gestation here are inconsistent with RDS, and surfactant is not indicated for TTN, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

new_options.text: Commence empirical IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin pending blood culture results and continue the current respiratory support level
new_options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics in a term infant with negative maternal GBS, no fever, no prolonged ROM and a CXR pattern characteristic of TTN are not routinely required; antibiotics are reserved for clinical concern for sepsis or persisting illness beyond 72 hours, per RCH CPG: Early onset sepsis.

new_options.text: Wean nasal high-flow off now and commence demand breastfeeding to encourage fetal lung-fluid clearance and feeding tolerance
new_options.rationale: Weaning support and commencing feeds while the infant remains in significant respiratory distress (RR 78, grunting, FiO2 0.30) risks aspiration and worsens work of breathing; nil by mouth and continued support are appropriate until respiratory effort settles, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

new_options.text: Urgently transfer to a tertiary neonatal intensive care unit for elective intubation and mechanical ventilation now
new_options.rationale: The infant is stable on non-invasive support with adequate saturations and a normal blood gas; intubation and tertiary transfer are reserved for failing non-invasive support, persistent hypoxaemia, or alternative diagnoses, per RCH CPG: Neonatal respiratory distress.

verdicts.new_stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman is reviewed in the antenatal clinic at 36 weeks gestation by the midwifery and obstetric team. She and her partner have a written birth plan declining intramuscular vitamin K for their baby because of concerns about historical claims linking IM vitamin K with childhood leukaemia. They are willing to consider oral vitamin K as a compromise but want to know the specific regimen and the residual risk. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated with reassuring serial growth and screening, the mother is on no medications (no anticonvulsants, antibiotics, rifampicin, or anticoagulants), and there are no risk factors for fetal coagulopathy or maternal liver disease. Their first child received IM vitamin K and had no problems. They intend to exclusively breastfeed and the booking is for a planned vaginal birth at 39 weeks. They specifically ask you to compare the oral and IM regimens by Australian guidance and have read that the leukaemia link has been refuted.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which counselling and recommendation is most appropriate?

new_options.text: IM vitamin K 1 mg single dose at birth first-line; if declined, oral 1 mg at birth, day 3-5, and 4 weeks with residual-risk counselling
new_options.rationale: NHMRC and RCH guidance recommends single-dose IM vitamin K 1 mg at birth as the most reliable prophylaxis against classical and late vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB). When IM is declined, the recommended oral alternative is THREE 1 mg oral doses (birth, day 3 to 5, and 4 weeks) - not a single oral dose - with documented informed consent including the higher residual late VKDB risk, particularly with exclusive breastfeeding and undetected cholestasis, per RCH CPG: Vitamin K prophylaxis and NHMRC Statement on Vitamin K.

new_options.text: Recommend a single oral 1 mg vitamin K dose at birth as an equivalent IM alternative with comparable late-VKDB protection
new_options.rationale: A single oral dose does not provide adequate protection against late VKDB; the recommended oral regimen requires three 1 mg doses (birth, day 3 to 5, 4 weeks), per RCH CPG: Vitamin K prophylaxis.

new_options.text: Recommend no vitamin K prophylaxis since the historical leukaemia link is refuted and modern Australian background bleeding risk is negligible
new_options.rationale: Vitamin K is required to prevent classical and late VKDB; the refutation of the leukaemia link does not eliminate the need for prophylaxis, and declining is not equivalent to safe practice, per RCH CPG: Vitamin K prophylaxis and NHMRC Statement on Vitamin K.

new_options.text: Recommend maternal oral vitamin K 20 mg daily from 36 weeks until delivery as a substitute for neonatal prophylaxis at birth
new_options.rationale: Maternal supplementation does not provide adequate neonatal vitamin K stores or protect against late VKDB and is not a recommended substitute, per NHMRC Statement on Vitamin K.

new_options.text: Defer the decision until after delivery so the family can meet the baby and decide with the postnatal midwife after birth
new_options.rationale: Antenatal informed consent allows the family to consider evidence and ask questions in a low-pressure setting; deferring this discussion to the immediate postnatal period is not best practice and risks unprepared decision-making, per RCH CPG: Vitamin K prophylaxis.
